summary: DHH argues that the twenty-year reign of SaaS is due for a correction, with software products (buy-once, self-hosted) poised for a resurgence. He outlines trade-offs from both the customer perspective—loss of data possession, forced upgrades, and perpetual subscriptions—and the vendor perspective—operational burden, obligation to tinker forever, and organizational mismatch between creation and maintenance. He points to Docker and similar breakthroughs as enabling easier distribution of installable software, and expresses a personal desire to both buy and sell finished software products rather than perpetual services.
key_points: SaaS customers sacrifice data possession, upgrade control, and the ability to pay once for finished software
SaaS vendors bear the burden of 24/7 operations, being a single point of failure, and the obligation to tinker with the same product forever
The creative energy of building new things is stifled by the service model's demand for perpetual maintenance
Technologies like Docker have dramatically eased software distribution, removing a key advantage SaaS held over installable products
The video game industry's historical separation of creative studios from publishers offers a model worth emulating
money_quotes: Possession in 9/10ths of the law, and with software services, you are just not in possession of your data.
Refusing service on individual moral, political, or other grounds is something people can do when they're offering a service, not after they've sold you a product.
Somehow we've all, buyers and sellers, swallowed the logic of planned obsolescence when it comes to software.
Most business software is bought because it serves a purpose. It's a tool, not entertainment. Novelty ought not be a top priority.
I wish there was a broad category of software that was offered in its final form.
Running a software service that has thousands of customers means you're the single point of failure for their dear data.

There's a reason many software makers pine for the rare opportunity to work on greenfield projects. That lush open space to start fresh with your best ideas, unencumbered by old promises and old decisions, is invigorating.
I dream of being able to both buy and sell finished business software products.
key_insight: The SaaS model imposes serious trade-offs on both customers and vendors, and with modern distribution tools like Docker removing the old hassles of installable software, the industry is ripe for a return to buy-once software products.
